title: PCOS
oneLiner: A common endocrine disorder in reproductive-aged women driven by insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism, causing ovulatory dysfunction, infertility, and long-term metabolic risks.

sections.Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS):
**One-liner:** A common endocrine disorder in reproductive-aged women driven by insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism, causing ovulatory dysfunction, infertility, and long-term metabolic risks.

---
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 24-year-old woman presents with a two-year history of irregular, infrequent periods (every 40–60 days) and increasing facial hair that she shaves weekly. She has struggled with weight gain since her teens (BMI 32 kg/m²) and notes oily skin with cystic acne along her jawline. On examination, you see coarse dark hairs on her upper lip, chin, and lower abdomen (Ferriman-Gallwey score 12), and you notice velvety, hyperpigmented patches on her neck and axillae.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Oligomenorrhea (cycles >35 days) | Anovulation – a cardinal feature of PCOS; indicates unopposed estrogen and no corpus luteum |
| Hirsutism (male-pattern hair growth) | Clinical hyperandrogenism; excess testosterone drives terminal hair growth in androgen-sensitive areas |
| Acne / oily skin (jawline distribution) | Androgen excess stimulates sebaceous glands; helps differentiate PCOS from idiopathic hirsutism |
| Central obesity (BMI >30) | Strong association with insulin resistance; weight gain both worsens and is worsened by IR |
| Acanthosis nigricans (neck, axillae) | Cutaneous marker of insulin resistance – a key pathophysiological driver in PCOS |
| Family history of type 2 diabetes or PCOS | Genetic predisposition; raises pretest probability of PCOS and metabolic syndrome |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Lean PCOS (~20% of cases):** Slim woman (BMI <25) with mild hirsutism and irregular periods – no obesity. Insulin resistance may be less obvious, but LH/FSH ratio often high. Missed if you rely only on BMI.
- **Adolescent with persistent acne and irregular periods:** Normal puberty can mimic PCOS. Key clue: persistence beyond 2–3 years post-menarche. If cycles are still >45 days, suspect PCOS.
- **Postmenopausal woman with new-onset hirsutism and virilization:** Rare; raises suspicion for androgen-secreting ovarian or adrenal tumor (differential). Ovarian hyperthecosis (severe form of PCOS) can also present late.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Polycystic Ovary Syndrome is a condition where the ovaries produce excess male hormones (androgens) due to a combination of insulin resistance and abnormal pituitary feedback. This disrupts the normal menstrual cycle, leading to infrequent or absent ovulation, and causes symptoms like unwanted hair growth, acne, and difficulty getting pregnant.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** PCOS affects 5–15% of women of reproductive age worldwide – it is the most common endocrine disorder in this group and the leading cause of anovulatory infertility.
- **Stakes:** Beyond fertility, PCOS carries lifelong metabolic risks – a 3–7 fold increased risk of type 2 diabetes, a doubling of cardiovascular disease risk, and a 3-fold increased risk of endometrial hyperplasia/cancer from unopposed estrogen.
- **Common Pitfall:** Many clinicians underdiagnose PCOS in lean women or adolescents, or overdiagnose in women who simply have polycystic ovaries on ultrasound without clinical features. Using established criteria (Rotterdam) is essential to avoid mislabelling normal variants.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story: Insulin Resistance is the Fire, Hyperandrogenism is the Smoke**  
Most women with PCOS have peripheral insulin resistance – their muscle and fat cells ignore insulin’s signal to take up glucose. The pancreas compensates by secreting more insulin, leading to **hyperinsulinemia**. Insulin, at high levels, becomes a co-gonadotropin: it binds to theca cells in the ovary and, together with luteinizing hormone (LH), drives excessive androgen production (testosterone, androstenedione). Simultaneously, insulin inhibits hepatic production of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), so more testosterone circulates freely – amplifying its effects.

Meanwhile, the hypothalamic-pituitary axis is disrupted: low progesterone from anovulation leads to loss of negative feedback, and estrogen feedback is unopposed, causing elevated LH pulse frequency and amplitude. The **LH/FSH ratio rises** (>2–3:1). High LH stimulates theca cells further, while relatively low FSH means follicles cannot mature to ovulation – they become arrested at the antral stage, forming the “polycystic” appearance on ultrasound.

**Why Each Key Sign Happens:**

| Pathophysiological Step | Clinical Sign |
|-------------------------|---------------|
| Hyperinsulinemia inhibits SHBG | Low SHBG → high free testosterone → hirsutism, acne, male-pattern alopecia |
| High insulin + LH → ovarian thecal androgen excess | Elevated total and free testosterone |
| Arrested follicular development (low FSH) | Oligo-ovulation → irregular menses, infertility |
| Unopposed estrogen from anovulation | Endometrial proliferation → risk of hyperplasia/cancer |
| Insulin resistance in peripheral tissues | Acanthosis nigricans, obesity (visceral fat), glucose intolerance |
| Elevated LH pulse frequency | LH/FSH ratio >2–3 on day 3–5 of cycle (if menstruating) |

**Connecting Pathophys to Pattern Recognition:**  
The classic vignette’s obesity exacerbates insulin resistance → more hyperinsulinemia → more androgens → worse hirsutism and anovulation. Acanthosis nigricans is a direct physical sign of insulin resistance. The irregular periods stem from anovulation – the ovary releases no egg, so no corpus luteum forms, and progesterone stays low. Without progesterone, the endometrium grows continuously under estrogen until it sheds unpredictably (breakthrough bleeding) or not at all (amenorrhea).

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific Cause | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic / Genetic** | Polygenic predisposition (variants in *DENND1A*, *THADA*, *LHCGR*) | “Sister/mother with PCOS or type 2 diabetes” |
| **Metabolic** | Insulin resistance (most common driver) | “Central obesity, acanthosis nigricans, family history of diabetes” |
| **Environmental** | Obesity (exacerbates insulin resistance) | “BMI >30, recent weight gain, sedentary lifestyle” |
| **Adrenal** | Excess DHEA-S (in some patients) | “Elevated DHEA-S without elevated cortisol or 17-OHP” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Use of valproate (for epilepsy) can induce PCOS-like phenotype | “Woman on valproate developing hirsutism and weight gain” |
| **Inflammatory** | Low-grade chronic inflammation (CRP, TNF-α) | “Elevated hs-CRP, associated with metabolic syndrome” |

**Most common cause:** Genetic predisposition + obesity-driven insulin resistance. PCOS is a syndrome with no single cause – the interplay between insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and abnormal gonadotropin secretion is central.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria (Rotterdam 2003):** Requires **2 of 3** features:
- Oligo- or anovulation (cycle length >35 days or <21 days, or amenorrhea)
- Clinical or biochemical signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, alopecia; elevated total or free testosterone)
- Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (ovarian volume >10 mL or ≥12 follicles 2–9 mm in one ovary)

**Must exclude:** Thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), androgen-secreting tumors.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Total testosterone, SHBG (calculate free androgen index: total T × 100 / SHBG)** | Hyperandrogenism | Confirm biochemical androgen excess | Free androgen index >5 suggests hyperandrogenism; low SHBG supports PCOS |
| **LH, FSH** | Pituitary function | Look for elevated LH/FSH ratio | LH/FSH >2–3 is classic but not required; LH alone >10 IU/L is suggestive |
| **17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP)** | Exclude late-onset CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Rule out mimic; 17-OHP >6 nmol/L may need ACTH stimulation test | Fasting 08:00 sample; if elevated, confirm with synacthen test |
| **Prolactin** | Exclude hyperprolactinemia | Rule out prolactinoma as cause of oligomenorrhea | >25 ng/mL needs workup; mildly elevated can occur in PCOS too |
| **TSH** | Exclude thyroid dysfunction | Rule out hypothyroidism causing menstrual disturbance | TSH >4.5 mIU/L suggests subclinical/overt hypothyroidism |
| **Transvaginal ultrasound** | Ovarian morphology | Look for polycystic ovaries | One ovary with volume >10 mL or ≥12 follicles (2–9 mm) |
| **HbA1c or OGTT (75g, fasting + 2h glucose)** | Glucose tolerance | Screen for prediabetes/diabetes (all women with PCOS) | HbA1c ≥5.7% = prediabetes; 2h glucose ≥7.8 mmol/L = IGT; ≥11.1 = diabetes |
| **Lipid profile** | Dyslipidemia | Assess cardiovascular risk | Often: low HDL, high triglycerides |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Single Finding Confirms / Rules Out?**  
No single test confirms PCOS. The combination of **oligo-ovulation + hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical)** in the absence of other disorders is highly specific. A **normal 17-OHP (<6 nmol/L)** effectively rules out late-onset CAH. A **normal ultrasound** does not exclude PCOS (especially in lean women).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management is **symptom-driven** – tailor to the patient’s primary concern (fertility, hirsutism, menstrual irregularity, metabolic health).

**Immediate (First Hour):**  
Not an acute emergency. For a newly diagnosed patient in clinic:
- Confirm diagnosis and exclude mimics.
- Initiate lifestyle counselling (weight loss, exercise, dietary modification) – this is **first-line for all**.
- **For infertility:** Refer to reproductive endocrinology; first-line ovulation induction is **clomiphene citrate 50–100 mg PO daily for 5 days (cycle days 2–6)** or **letrozole 2.5–7.5 mg PO daily for 5 days**. Monitor with ultrasound.

**Ward (24–72h):**  
PCOS rarely requires hospital admission unless for complications (e.g., heavy menstrual bleeding with anaemia, DKA from diabetes, or ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome from treatment). If needed:
- **Heavy bleeding:** Tranexamic acid 1g IV/PO three times daily for up to 5 days; if severe, high-dose oestrogen (conjugated oestrogen 25 mg IV q4-6h) or combined OCP.
- **Diabetes/IGT:** Start metformin **500 mg PO BID** (titrate over 2 weeks to 1g BID or 2g total daily). Monitor glucose.

**Long-Term (Primary Care / Ongoing):**

| Concern | Treatment | Doses & Route | Duration |
|---------|-----------|---------------|----------|
| **Hirsutism / Acne** | Combined oral contraceptive (COC) – estrogen + antiandrogenic progestin (e.g., drospirenone, cyproterone) | One tablet daily for 21/7 cycle | At least 6 months for hair improvement; indefinite if needed |
| **Hirsutism (if COC contraindicated)** | Spironolactone | 50–100 mg PO daily (titrate) | Continuous; monitor K+ in renal disease |
| **Anovulation / Infertility** | Letrozole (1st line) or clomiphene | Letrozole 2.5–7.5 mg daily days 2–6; clomiphene 50–100 mg daily days 2–6 | Ovulatory cycles; max 6–12 cycles |
| **Metabolic (IGT, diabetes prevention)** | Metformin | 500–1000 mg PO BID (max 2g) | Indefinite if prediabetes/diabetes; also consider for ovulation if clomiphene-resistant |
| **Endometrial protection** | Consider cyclical progestogen (e.g., dydrogesterone 10 mg PO days 12–25) if not on COC | 10 mg once daily for 14 days each month | Prevents hyperplasia; continue until cycles regulated or menopause |
| **Weight loss** | Lifestyle modification ± GLP-1 agonists (e.g., liraglutide, semaglutide) if BMI >30 | Variable; liraglutide 3 mg daily SC for weight | Long-term |

**Common Pitfalls – What NOT to Do:**
- **DO NOT** prescribe metformin as first-line for hirsutism – it helps modestly but COC is far superior.
- **DO NOT** use OCP in women actively trying to conceive – switch to ovulation induction.
- **DO NOT** rely on metformin alone for infertility – evidence shows letrozole and clomiphene are more effective for ovulation.
- **DO NOT** diagnose PCOS in adolescents based solely on ultrasound (polycystic-appearing ovaries are normal in ~50% of teenagers). Use stricter criteria: persistent oligomenorrhea + clinical hyperandrogenism.
- **DO NOT** forget to screen for type 2 diabetes and lipid abnormalities in all women with PCOS – many clinicians focus only on cosmetic concerns.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | PCOS | Late-Onset CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Cushing’s Syndrome | Hyperprolactinemia |
|---------|------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Menstrual pattern** | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea (often galactorrhea) |
| **Hyperandrogenism** | Common (hirsutism, acne, alopecia) | Common (often more severe, early onset) | Variable (acne, hirsutism, but often milder) | Uncommon (can occur due to DHEA from ACTH) |
| **Virilization** | Rare (mild if present; suggests tumor) | Mild to moderate | Mild to moderate | Absent |
| **Cortisol / Cushing features** | Normal | Normal | Elevated urinary free cortisol, 1mg dexamethasone suppression test >50 nmol/L | Normal |
| **Prolactin** | Normal or mildly elevated | Normal | Normal | Elevated >25 ng/mL (often >100) |
| **17-OHP** | Normal | Elevated (>6 nmol/L fasting; >30 nmol/L after ACTH) | Normal | Normal |
| **Imaging** | Ovaries: volume >10 mL or ≥12 follicles | Adrenal hyperplasia on CT (rarely) | Pituitary adenoma (MRI) or adrenal mass | Pituitary adenoma (MRI) |
| **Key Differentiator** | Insulin resistance markers; LH/FSH ratio >2; absence of virilization | 17-OHP levels; family history of CAH; 21-hydroxylase gene mutation | Cushingoid habitus, cortisol excess, central obesity | Galactorrhea, prolactin level, pituitary MRI findings |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complications and How They Present:**

1. **Endometrial Hyperplasia / Cancer**  
   - **Presentation:** Heavy, prolonged, or intermenstrual bleeding in a woman with long-standing oligomenorrhea.  
   - **Key signs:** Anaemia, thickened endometrium on ultrasound (>5 mm in postmenopausal or >12 mm in reproductive age with disordered pattern).  
   - **What to do:** Endometrial biopsy (pipelle). Ensure progestogen treatment or COC for protection.

2. **Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus**  
   - **Presentation:** Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (or asymptomatic).  
   - **Key signs:** HbA1c ≥6.5% or fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L or 2h glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L on OGTT.  
   - **What to do:** Start metformin, lifestyle counselling, referral to diabetes clinic. Screen annually if IGT.

3. **Cardiovascular Disease**  
   - **Presentation:** Premature coronary artery disease, stroke before age 50.  
   - **Risk factors:** Low HDL, high triglycerides, obesity, hypertension, insulin resistance.  
   - **What to do:** Aggressive lipid and BP management; statin if LDL >2.6 mmol/L and age >40 or multiple risk factors.

4. **Infertility**  
   - **Presentation:** Inability to conceive after 12 months of trying.  
   - **What to do:** Refer for ovulation induction – if left untreated, anovulatory infertility persists.

5. **Sleep Apnoea**  
   - **Presentation:** Daytime somnolence, snoring, morning headache.  
   - **What to do:** Screen with STOP-BANG questionnaire; refer for polysomnography.

6. **Non-alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD)**  
   - **Presentation:** Often asymptomatic; elevated ALT; hepatic steatosis on ultrasound.  
   - **What to do:** Weight loss, metformin may help; avoid hepatotoxins; monitor for fibrosis.

**Late-Presentation Patterns (Exam Stems):**  
- A 45-year-old woman with a 20-year history of untreated PCOS presents with postmenopausal bleeding – investigate for endometrial cancer.  
- A 38-year-old woman with PCOS develops polydipsia and polyuria – test for new-onset diabetes.  
- A lean woman with normal periods but infertility – check androgen levels and insulin resistance; lean PCOS is often missed.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for Rotterdam Diagnostic Criteria:**  
**“HOP”** – **H**yperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), **O**ligo-ovulation, **P**olycystic ovaries on ultrasound (need 2 of 3).

**Mnemonic for PCOS Long-Term Surveillance:**  
**“ABCs”** – **A**ndrogen control (hirsutism), **B**leeding pattern (endometrial protection), **C**ardiovascular risk (DM, lipids), **S**leep apnoea and **S**kin (acanthosis).

**Clinical Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues):**

1. **Acanthosis nigricans is a red flag for insulin resistance** – if present, you must screen for diabetes, regardless of BMI.
2. **LH/FSH ratio >2 supports PCOS** but is not diagnostic – up to 40% of women with PCOS have a normal ratio.
3. **Weight loss of 5–10% restores ovulation in ~50% of women** – always counsel before starting fertility drugs.
4. **COC is first-line for hirsutism if not trying to conceive** – spironolactone is second-line and needs contraception.
5. **17-OHP must be measured fasting in the morning** to avoid false elevations – if borderline, do ACTH stimulation test.
6. **Adolescents: do not diagnose PCOS based solely on ultrasound or a single cycle** – menstrual irregularity is common in first 2–3 years post-menarche.
7. **If a woman with PCOS develops severe virilization (clitoromegaly, deepening voice, rapid-onset hirsutism)** – think androgen-secreting tumor (ovarian or adrenal) and refer urgently for imaging.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Ordering an ultrasound to “rule out PCOS” – a normal scan does not exclude PCOS (especially in thin women).
- Prescribing metformin as a first-line fertility drug – it’s inferior to letrozole/clomiphene for ovulation induction.
- Forgetting to check fasting glucose or OGTT in a woman with PCOS, even if she is lean.
- Using OCP in a woman with PCOS who is trying to conceive – switch to ovulation induction.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / Refer:**

- **Suspected androgen-secreting tumor:** Rapid-onset virilization, total testosterone >5 nmol/L (twice upper limit), DHEA-S > 20 μmol/L, or palpable adnexal mass → urgent gynaecology/oncology referral.
- **Severe endometrial bleeding with haemodynamic instability:** Hb <80 g/L, tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension → admit, discuss with gynaecology for urgent biopsy and possibly dilatation and curettage.
- **New-onset diabetes with ketosis or hyperosmolar state:** Capillary glucose >15 mmol/L, ketones >1.5 mmol/L, or pH <7.3 → admit for insulin therapy and fluid resuscitation; escalate to diabetes team.
- **Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) from ovulation induction:** Abdominal pain, nausea, oliguria, weight gain >5 kg in 5 days, haematocrit >45% → stop gonadotropins, call reproductive endocrinology, consider admission for IV fluids and paracentesis.

**Specific Thresholds:**

| Parameter | Threshold for Escalation |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| NEWS2 | ≥5 (or any single parameter ≥3) → call outreach/ICU |
| Lactate | >2.0 mmol/L (consider sepsis from endometrial infection) |
| GCS | <15 or acute confusion → CT head, neurology review (very rare) |
| Hb | <70 g/L (symptomatic) → consider transfusion |
| K+ | >5.5 mmol/L (if on spironolactone) → stop drug, check renal function |
| Glucose | >22 mmol/L with ketones → DKA protocol |

**When to Involve Multidisciplinary Team:**  
- Fertility issues → reproductive endocrinology  
- Complex metabolic syndrome (DM, NAFLD, hypertension) → endocrinology ± dietician  
- Hirsutism resistant to COC → dermatology (laser, electrolysis)  
- Endometrial hyperplasia → gynaecology for hysteroscopy and surveillance  
- Sleep apnoea → respiratory medicine for polysomnogram  

**Bottom Line for the Junior Doctor:**  
PCOS is a lifelong condition

rawMarkdown:
## Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
**One-liner:** A common endocrine disorder in reproductive-aged women driven by insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism, causing ovulatory dysfunction, infertility, and long-term metabolic risks.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 24-year-old woman presents with a two-year history of irregular, infrequent periods (every 40–60 days) and increasing facial hair that she shaves weekly. She has struggled with weight gain since her teens (BMI 32 kg/m²) and notes oily skin with cystic acne along her jawline. On examination, you see coarse dark hairs on her upper lip, chin, and lower abdomen (Ferriman-Gallwey score 12), and you notice velvety, hyperpigmented patches on her neck and axillae.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Oligomenorrhea (cycles >35 days) | Anovulation – a cardinal feature of PCOS; indicates unopposed estrogen and no corpus luteum |
| Hirsutism (male-pattern hair growth) | Clinical hyperandrogenism; excess testosterone drives terminal hair growth in androgen-sensitive areas |
| Acne / oily skin (jawline distribution) | Androgen excess stimulates sebaceous glands; helps differentiate PCOS from idiopathic hirsutism |
| Central obesity (BMI >30) | Strong association with insulin resistance; weight gain both worsens and is worsened by IR |
| Acanthosis nigricans (neck, axillae) | Cutaneous marker of insulin resistance – a key pathophysiological driver in PCOS |
| Family history of type 2 diabetes or PCOS | Genetic predisposition; raises pretest probability of PCOS and metabolic syndrome |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Lean PCOS (~20% of cases):** Slim woman (BMI <25) with mild hirsutism and irregular periods – no obesity. Insulin resistance may be less obvious, but LH/FSH ratio often high. Missed if you rely only on BMI.
- **Adolescent with persistent acne and irregular periods:** Normal puberty can mimic PCOS. Key clue: persistence beyond 2–3 years post-menarche. If cycles are still >45 days, suspect PCOS.
- **Postmenopausal woman with new-onset hirsutism and virilization:** Rare; raises suspicion for androgen-secreting ovarian or adrenal tumor (differential). Ovarian hyperthecosis (severe form of PCOS) can also present late.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Polycystic Ovary Syndrome is a condition where the ovaries produce excess male hormones (androgens) due to a combination of insulin resistance and abnormal pituitary feedback. This disrupts the normal menstrual cycle, leading to infrequent or absent ovulation, and causes symptoms like unwanted hair growth, acne, and difficulty getting pregnant.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** PCOS affects 5–15% of women of reproductive age worldwide – it is the most common endocrine disorder in this group and the leading cause of anovulatory infertility.
- **Stakes:** Beyond fertility, PCOS carries lifelong metabolic risks – a 3–7 fold increased risk of type 2 diabetes, a doubling of cardiovascular disease risk, and a 3-fold increased risk of endometrial hyperplasia/cancer from unopposed estrogen.
- **Common Pitfall:** Many clinicians underdiagnose PCOS in lean women or adolescents, or overdiagnose in women who simply have polycystic ovaries on ultrasound without clinical features. Using established criteria (Rotterdam) is essential to avoid mislabelling normal variants.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story: Insulin Resistance is the Fire, Hyperandrogenism is the Smoke**  
Most women with PCOS have peripheral insulin resistance – their muscle and fat cells ignore insulin’s signal to take up glucose. The pancreas compensates by secreting more insulin, leading to **hyperinsulinemia**. Insulin, at high levels, becomes a co-gonadotropin: it binds to theca cells in the ovary and, together with luteinizing hormone (LH), drives excessive androgen production (testosterone, androstenedione). Simultaneously, insulin inhibits hepatic production of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), so more testosterone circulates freely – amplifying its effects.

Meanwhile, the hypothalamic-pituitary axis is disrupted: low progesterone from anovulation leads to loss of negative feedback, and estrogen feedback is unopposed, causing elevated LH pulse frequency and amplitude. The **LH/FSH ratio rises** (>2–3:1). High LH stimulates theca cells further, while relatively low FSH means follicles cannot mature to ovulation – they become arrested at the antral stage, forming the “polycystic” appearance on ultrasound.

**Why Each Key Sign Happens:**

| Pathophysiological Step | Clinical Sign |
|-------------------------|---------------|
| Hyperinsulinemia inhibits SHBG | Low SHBG → high free testosterone → hirsutism, acne, male-pattern alopecia |
| High insulin + LH → ovarian thecal androgen excess | Elevated total and free testosterone |
| Arrested follicular development (low FSH) | Oligo-ovulation → irregular menses, infertility |
| Unopposed estrogen from anovulation | Endometrial proliferation → risk of hyperplasia/cancer |
| Insulin resistance in peripheral tissues | Acanthosis nigricans, obesity (visceral fat), glucose intolerance |
| Elevated LH pulse frequency | LH/FSH ratio >2–3 on day 3–5 of cycle (if menstruating) |

**Connecting Pathophys to Pattern Recognition:**  
The classic vignette’s obesity exacerbates insulin resistance → more hyperinsulinemia → more androgens → worse hirsutism and anovulation. Acanthosis nigricans is a direct physical sign of insulin resistance. The irregular periods stem from anovulation – the ovary releases no egg, so no corpus luteum forms, and progesterone stays low. Without progesterone, the endometrium grows continuously under estrogen until it sheds unpredictably (breakthrough bleeding) or not at all (amenorrhea).

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Specific Cause | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic / Genetic** | Polygenic predisposition (variants in *DENND1A*, *THADA*, *LHCGR*) | “Sister/mother with PCOS or type 2 diabetes” |
| **Metabolic** | Insulin resistance (most common driver) | “Central obesity, acanthosis nigricans, family history of diabetes” |
| **Environmental** | Obesity (exacerbates insulin resistance) | “BMI >30, recent weight gain, sedentary lifestyle” |
| **Adrenal** | Excess DHEA-S (in some patients) | “Elevated DHEA-S without elevated cortisol or 17-OHP” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Use of valproate (for epilepsy) can induce PCOS-like phenotype | “Woman on valproate developing hirsutism and weight gain” |
| **Inflammatory** | Low-grade chronic inflammation (CRP, TNF-α) | “Elevated hs-CRP, associated with metabolic syndrome” |

**Most common cause:** Genetic predisposition + obesity-driven insulin resistance. PCOS is a syndrome with no single cause – the interplay between insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and abnormal gonadotropin secretion is central.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria (Rotterdam 2003):** Requires **2 of 3** features:
- Oligo- or anovulation (cycle length >35 days or <21 days, or amenorrhea)
- Clinical or biochemical signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, alopecia; elevated total or free testosterone)
- Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (ovarian volume >10 mL or ≥12 follicles 2–9 mm in one ovary)

**Must exclude:** Thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), androgen-secreting tumors.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Total testosterone, SHBG (calculate free androgen index: total T × 100 / SHBG)** | Hyperandrogenism | Confirm biochemical androgen excess | Free androgen index >5 suggests hyperandrogenism; low SHBG supports PCOS |
| **LH, FSH** | Pituitary function | Look for elevated LH/FSH ratio | LH/FSH >2–3 is classic but not required; LH alone >10 IU/L is suggestive |
| **17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP)** | Exclude late-onset CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Rule out mimic; 17-OHP >6 nmol/L may need ACTH stimulation test | Fasting 08:00 sample; if elevated, confirm with synacthen test |
| **Prolactin** | Exclude hyperprolactinemia | Rule out prolactinoma as cause of oligomenorrhea | >25 ng/mL needs workup; mildly elevated can occur in PCOS too |
| **TSH** | Exclude thyroid dysfunction | Rule out hypothyroidism causing menstrual disturbance | TSH >4.5 mIU/L suggests subclinical/overt hypothyroidism |
| **Transvaginal ultrasound** | Ovarian morphology | Look for polycystic ovaries | One ovary with volume >10 mL or ≥12 follicles (2–9 mm) |
| **HbA1c or OGTT (75g, fasting + 2h glucose)** | Glucose tolerance | Screen for prediabetes/diabetes (all women with PCOS) | HbA1c ≥5.7% = prediabetes; 2h glucose ≥7.8 mmol/L = IGT; ≥11.1 = diabetes |
| **Lipid profile** | Dyslipidemia | Assess cardiovascular risk | Often: low HDL, high triglycerides |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Single Finding Confirms / Rules Out?**  
No single test confirms PCOS. The combination of **oligo-ovulation + hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical)** in the absence of other disorders is highly specific. A **normal 17-OHP (<6 nmol/L)** effectively rules out late-onset CAH. A **normal ultrasound** does not exclude PCOS (especially in lean women).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management is **symptom-driven** – tailor to the patient’s primary concern (fertility, hirsutism, menstrual irregularity, metabolic health).

**Immediate (First Hour):**  
Not an acute emergency. For a newly diagnosed patient in clinic:
- Confirm diagnosis and exclude mimics.
- Initiate lifestyle counselling (weight loss, exercise, dietary modification) – this is **first-line for all**.
- **For infertility:** Refer to reproductive endocrinology; first-line ovulation induction is **clomiphene citrate 50–100 mg PO daily for 5 days (cycle days 2–6)** or **letrozole 2.5–7.5 mg PO daily for 5 days**. Monitor with ultrasound.

**Ward (24–72h):**  
PCOS rarely requires hospital admission unless for complications (e.g., heavy menstrual bleeding with anaemia, DKA from diabetes, or ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome from treatment). If needed:
- **Heavy bleeding:** Tranexamic acid 1g IV/PO three times daily for up to 5 days; if severe, high-dose oestrogen (conjugated oestrogen 25 mg IV q4-6h) or combined OCP.
- **Diabetes/IGT:** Start metformin **500 mg PO BID** (titrate over 2 weeks to 1g BID or 2g total daily). Monitor glucose.

**Long-Term (Primary Care / Ongoing):**

| Concern | Treatment | Doses & Route | Duration |
|---------|-----------|---------------|----------|
| **Hirsutism / Acne** | Combined oral contraceptive (COC) – estrogen + antiandrogenic progestin (e.g., drospirenone, cyproterone) | One tablet daily for 21/7 cycle | At least 6 months for hair improvement; indefinite if needed |
| **Hirsutism (if COC contraindicated)** | Spironolactone | 50–100 mg PO daily (titrate) | Continuous; monitor K+ in renal disease |
| **Anovulation / Infertility** | Letrozole (1st line) or clomiphene | Letrozole 2.5–7.5 mg daily days 2–6; clomiphene 50–100 mg daily days 2–6 | Ovulatory cycles; max 6–12 cycles |
| **Metabolic (IGT, diabetes prevention)** | Metformin | 500–1000 mg PO BID (max 2g) | Indefinite if prediabetes/diabetes; also consider for ovulation if clomiphene-resistant |
| **Endometrial protection** | Consider cyclical progestogen (e.g., dydrogesterone 10 mg PO days 12–25) if not on COC | 10 mg once daily for 14 days each month | Prevents hyperplasia; continue until cycles regulated or menopause |
| **Weight loss** | Lifestyle modification ± GLP-1 agonists (e.g., liraglutide, semaglutide) if BMI >30 | Variable; liraglutide 3 mg daily SC for weight | Long-term |

**Common Pitfalls – What NOT to Do:**
- **DO NOT** prescribe metformin as first-line for hirsutism – it helps modestly but COC is far superior.
- **DO NOT** use OCP in women actively trying to conceive – switch to ovulation induction.
- **DO NOT** rely on metformin alone for infertility – evidence shows letrozole and clomiphene are more effective for ovulation.
- **DO NOT** diagnose PCOS in adolescents based solely on ultrasound (polycystic-appearing ovaries are normal in ~50% of teenagers). Use stricter criteria: persistent oligomenorrhea + clinical hyperandrogenism.
- **DO NOT** forget to screen for type 2 diabetes and lipid abnormalities in all women with PCOS – many clinicians focus only on cosmetic concerns.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | PCOS | Late-Onset CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Cushing’s Syndrome | Hyperprolactinemia |
|---------|------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Menstrual pattern** | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea | Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea (often galactorrhea) |
| **Hyperandrogenism** | Common (hirsutism, acne, alopecia) | Common (often more severe, early onset) | Variable (acne, hirsutism, but often milder) | Uncommon (can occur due to DHEA from ACTH) |
| **Virilization** | Rare (mild if present; suggests tumor) | Mild to moderate | Mild to moderate | Absent |
| **Cortisol / Cushing features** | Normal | Normal | Elevated urinary free cortisol, 1mg dexamethasone suppression test >50 nmol/L | Normal |
| **Prolactin** | Normal or mildly elevated | Normal | Normal | Elevated >25 ng/mL (often >100) |
| **17-OHP** | Normal | Elevated (>6 nmol/L fasting; >30 nmol/L after ACTH) | Normal | Normal |
| **Imaging** | Ovaries: volume >10 mL or ≥12 follicles | Adrenal hyperplasia on CT (rarely) | Pituitary adenoma (MRI) or adrenal mass | Pituitary adenoma (MRI) |
| **Key Differentiator** | Insulin resistance markers; LH/FSH ratio >2; absence of virilization | 17-OHP levels; family history of CAH; 21-hydroxylase gene mutation | Cushingoid habitus, cortisol excess, central obesity | Galactorrhea, prolactin level, pituitary MRI findings |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major Complications and How They Present:**

1. **Endometrial Hyperplasia / Cancer**  
   - **Presentation:** Heavy, prolonged, or intermenstrual bleeding in a woman with long-standing oligomenorrhea.  
   - **Key signs:** Anaemia, thickened endometrium on ultrasound (>5 mm in postmenopausal or >12 mm in reproductive age with disordered pattern).  
   - **What to do:** Endometrial biopsy (pipelle). Ensure progestogen treatment or COC for protection.

2. **Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus**  
   - **Presentation:** Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (or asymptomatic).  
   - **Key signs:** HbA1c ≥6.5% or fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L or 2h glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L on OGTT.  
   - **What to do:** Start metformin, lifestyle counselling, referral to diabetes clinic. Screen annually if IGT.

3. **Cardiovascular Disease**  
   - **Presentation:** Premature coronary artery disease, stroke before age 50.  
   - **Risk factors:** Low HDL, high triglycerides, obesity, hypertension, insulin resistance.  
   - **What to do:** Aggressive lipid and BP management; statin if LDL >2.6 mmol/L and age >40 or multiple risk factors.

4. **Infertility**  
   - **Presentation:** Inability to conceive after 12 months of trying.  
   - **What to do:** Refer for ovulation induction – if left untreated, anovulatory infertility persists.

5. **Sleep Apnoea**  
   - **Presentation:** Daytime somnolence, snoring, morning headache.  
   - **What to do:** Screen with STOP-BANG questionnaire; refer for polysomnography.

6. **Non-alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD)**  
   - **Presentation:** Often asymptomatic; elevated ALT; hepatic steatosis on ultrasound.  
   - **What to do:** Weight loss, metformin may help; avoid hepatotoxins; monitor for fibrosis.

**Late-Presentation Patterns (Exam Stems):**  
- A 45-year-old woman with a 20-year history of untreated PCOS presents with postmenopausal bleeding – investigate for endometrial cancer.  
- A 38-year-old woman with PCOS develops polydipsia and polyuria – test for new-onset diabetes.  
- A lean woman with normal periods but infertility – check androgen levels and insulin resistance; lean PCOS is often missed.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for Rotterdam Diagnostic Criteria:**  
**“HOP”** – **H**yperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), **O**ligo-ovulation, **P**olycystic ovaries on ultrasound (need 2 of 3).

**Mnemonic for PCOS Long-Term Surveillance:**  
**“ABCs”** – **A**ndrogen control (hirsutism), **B**leeding pattern (endometrial protection), **C**ardiovascular risk (DM, lipids), **S**leep apnoea and **S**kin (acanthosis).

**Clinical Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues):**

1. **Acanthosis nigricans is a red flag for insulin resistance** – if present, you must screen for diabetes, regardless of BMI.
2. **LH/FSH ratio >2 supports PCOS** but is not diagnostic – up to 40% of women with PCOS have a normal ratio.
3. **Weight loss of 5–10% restores ovulation in ~50% of women** – always counsel before starting fertility drugs.
4. **COC is first-line for hirsutism if not trying to conceive** – spironolactone is second-line and needs contraception.
5. **17-OHP must be measured fasting in the morning** to avoid false elevations – if borderline, do ACTH stimulation test.
6. **Adolescents: do not diagnose PCOS based solely on ultrasound or a single cycle** – menstrual irregularity is common in first 2–3 years post-menarche.
7. **If a woman with PCOS develops severe virilization (clitoromegaly, deepening voice, rapid-onset hirsutism)** – think androgen-secreting tumor (ovarian or adrenal) and refer urgently for imaging.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Ordering an ultrasound to “rule out PCOS” – a normal scan does not exclude PCOS (especially in thin women).
- Prescribing metformin as a first-line fertility drug – it’s inferior to letrozole/clomiphene for ovulation induction.
- Forgetting to check fasting glucose or OGTT in a woman with PCOS, even if she is lean.
- Using OCP in a woman with PCOS who is trying to conceive – switch to ovulation induction.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / Refer:**

- **Suspected androgen-secreting tumor:** Rapid-onset virilization, total testosterone >5 nmol/L (twice upper limit), DHEA-S > 20 μmol/L, or palpable adnexal mass → urgent gynaecology/oncology referral.
- **Severe endometrial bleeding with haemodynamic instability:** Hb <80 g/L, tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension → admit, discuss with gynaecology for urgent biopsy and possibly dilatation and curettage.
- **New-onset diabetes with ketosis or hyperosmolar state:** Capillary glucose >15 mmol/L, ketones >1.5 mmol/L, or pH <7.3 → admit for insulin therapy and fluid resuscitation; escalate to diabetes team.
- **Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) from ovulation induction:** Abdominal pain, nausea, oliguria, weight gain >5 kg in 5 days, haematocrit >45% → stop gonadotropins, call reproductive endocrinology, consider admission for IV fluids and paracentesis.

**Specific Thresholds:**

| Parameter | Threshold for Escalation |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| NEWS2 | ≥5 (or any single parameter ≥3) → call outreach/ICU |
| Lactate | >2.0 mmol/L (consider sepsis from endometrial infection) |
| GCS | <15 or acute confusion → CT head, neurology review (very rare) |
| Hb | <70 g/L (symptomatic) → consider transfusion |
| K+ | >5.5 mmol/L (if on spironolactone) → stop drug, check renal function |
| Glucose | >22 mmol/L with ketones → DKA protocol |

**When to Involve Multidisciplinary Team:**  
- Fertility issues → reproductive endocrinology  
- Complex metabolic syndrome (DM, NAFLD, hypertension) → endocrinology ± dietician  
- Hirsutism resistant to COC → dermatology (laser, electrolysis)  
- Endometrial hyperplasia → gynaecology for hysteroscopy and surveillance  
- Sleep apnoea → respiratory medicine for polysomnogram  

**Bottom Line for the Junior Doctor:**  
PCOS is a lifelong condition
